title: Quantifying modern slavery risk in photovoltaic supply chains
text:
Abstract
The rapid scaling of solar photovoltaic (PV) deployment has surfaced a paradox: the same supply chains driving the energy transition also carry significant exposure to forced-labour risk, particularly in upstream polysilicon refining. We combine bilateral trade data, facility-level audits, and labour-rights indices to quantify modern-slavery risk across the global PV supply chain.

Water Societal Metabolism in the Yucatán Peninsula
text: ← Back to Projects

title: Water Societal Metabolism in the Yucatán Peninsula
text:
Overview
The Yucatán Peninsula sits atop one of the world’s largest karst aquifer systems — but its groundwater is both the primary source of freshwater for the region and acutely vulnerable to the dual pressures of economic expansion and climate change. As tourism and agribusiness have grown, water demand has scaled in ways that simple per-capita indicators cannot capture. At the same time, climate projections suggest meaningful reductions in the rainfall that recharges the aquifer.
This project applies MuSIASEM (Multi-Scale Integrated Assessment of Societal and Ecosystem Metabolism) to connect regional socio-economic structure — who is doing what kind of work, in which sectors, at what scale — to water consumption, and then confronts that demand against biophysical constraints from groundwater recharge modelling. The result is a framework that makes visible the structural drivers of water scarcity, rather than treating consumption as a fixed coefficient of population.
The work formed the core of my doctoral thesis at the Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya (UPC), supervised by Martí Rosas-Casals and Laura M. Hernández-Terrones.

title: Water Societal Metabolism in the Yucatán Peninsula
text:
Methods

Water supply: groundwater recharge modelling
A monthly water balance model was calibrated against 40 years of historical data (1961–2000), capturing the strong seasonality of rainfall and evapotranspiration in the peninsula’s tropical climate. Four downscaled General Circulation Models provided climate projections for 2015–2039, enabling an ensemble estimate of future recharge under different emissions trajectories.
Key result: historical groundwater recharge of 118 ± 33 mm·year⁻¹ (~10 % of annual precipitation), with projections indicating approximately a 23 % decline by 2030 under current socio-economic and climate trajectories.


Water demand: MuSIASEM
MuSIASEM models an economy as a set of nested compartments — household, paid work, and the various productive sectors — and tracks how energy, water, and labour flow between them. Unlike input–output models, it enforces biophysical accounting constraints and is sensitive to the composition of economic activity, not just its aggregate size.
For each sub-region of the Yucatán Peninsula, demand scenarios were constructed by combining:

Employment structure by sector (agriculture, tourism, industry, services)
Water intensity coefficients by sector and activity type
Demographic projections under alternative development pathways

This revealed superlinear scaling between water metabolic rate and total water consumption: as the economy grows and diversifies towards high water-intensity activities, demand grows faster than population.


Integration
Supply (recharge) and demand (MuSIASEM scenarios) were compared at the sub-regional level, identifying where and when demand approaches or exceeds natural recharge limits — a condition two of the four scenarios reach by 2030.

title: Water Societal Metabolism in the Yucatán Peninsula
text:
Key findings

Under current trends, two of four climate-economic scenarios project water demand reaching or exceeding natural groundwater recharge by 2030 in at least one sub-region.
Aggregate per-capita indicators mask significant variation between sub-regions with different economic compositions; policy responses need to be geographically differentiated.
Tourism-heavy coastal zones show disproportionately high water intensity relative to their share of population, creating spatial imbalances in aquifer drawdown.
A 23 % projected decline in recharge combines with demand growth to produce a scissors effect — the crossing point is not a distant abstraction but a near-term planning horizon.

title: Water Societal Metabolism in the Yucatán Peninsula
text:
Why societal metabolism?
Standard water management models treat demand as a function of population and fixed sector coefficients. MuSIASEM treats the economy as a metabolic system: its structure determines how intensively each unit of output consumes water, and structural change (a shift from subsistence agriculture to resort tourism, for example) changes the metabolic profile even if population stays constant. This makes the approach sensitive to the kinds of development transitions actually underway in the Yucatán — and elsewhere in the Global South.

title: CLIDEWO: Climate Change, Decent Work and Workers’ Health in Brazilian Agriculture
text: ← Back to Projects

title: CLIDEWO: Climate Change, Decent Work and Workers’ Health in Brazilian Agriculture
text:
Overview
Agricultural workers in Brazil face a compounding set of risks: climate change is intensifying heat, drought, and seasonal extremes across key farming regions at the same time as poor working conditions — including wage theft, bonded labour, and exposure to pesticides — leave workers without the protections or income buffers to adapt. CLIDEWO asks how these pressures interact: where are workers most exposed, which climate hazards most directly worsen labour conditions, and what do workers themselves identify as the most urgent risks?
The project conducts a worker-informed, data-driven climate risk and vulnerability assessment of agricultural workers in Brazil, combining quantitative climate and socio-economic modelling with systematic collection of worker voice data. Its aim is to support policy action on SDG 8 (decent work) in the context of accelerating climate change.

title: CLIDEWO: Climate Change, Decent Work and Workers’ Health in Brazilian Agriculture
text:
Methods
CLIDEWO uses a multi-layer risk assessment approach that integrates three types of evidence:
Climate models Downscaled climate projections for key agricultural regions of Brazil, identifying exposure to heat stress, drought, extreme rainfall, and other hazards relevant to outdoor agricultural labour.
Socio-economic data National labour market statistics, sectoral employment data, and existing administrative records on labour inspection outcomes, combined with geographic information on agricultural production systems.
Qualitative worker voice data Structured engagement with agricultural workers across partner regions, capturing worker perceptions of risk, adaptive capacity, and the specific ways in which climate and working conditions intersect in their daily experience.
These layers are integrated into a composite vulnerability assessment that identifies the populations and locations of greatest concern, and supports scenario analysis for policy recommendations.

title: Granted water volume — Mexico (REPDA 2017)
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Mexico allocates water rights through the REPDA (Registro Público de Derechos de Agua), the national registry that records who holds concessions, for how much volume, from which source, and for which purpose. The 2017 data covers the full national registry — agriculture, industry, public supply, and other uses — split between surface water and groundwater sources.
This dashboard was built to understand how total allocated volume compares to natural availability at state and basin level, and to put the Yucatán Peninsula’s water situation in national context. The peninsula’s dependence on a single karst aquifer makes its water rights picture quite different from states with river systems: virtually all concessions draw from groundwater, and the aquifer has no buffer.

title: Granted water volume — Mexico (REPDA 2017)
text:
How to read it
Filters allow exploration by state, usage type, and water source. Volume figures are in cubic metres per year. Agricultural use dominates nationally; in Yucatán, it competes with the tourism sector’s rapidly growing demand.

title: Granted water volume — Mexico (REPDA 2017)
text:

title: Mobility analysis — Mexico (COVID-19 period)
text:
← Back to Portfolio
When Mexico declared a health emergency in March 2020, movement across the country changed — but not uniformly. Some states saw sharp drops in mobility from the first week. Others barely shifted. The variation tracked differences in local economic structure (where working from home was possible versus where it was not), local government messaging, and the pace at which the virus reached different regions.
This dashboard tracks those changes over time by state, using aggregated mobility indicators relative to a pre-pandemic baseline. The patterns in the data connect to larger questions about which populations were able to reduce their exposure and which were not — a question directly relevant to understanding how public health measures interact with labour vulnerability and informality.

title: Mobility analysis — Mexico (COVID-19 period)
text:
How to read it
Values are percentage changes from the baseline period (January–February 2020). Negative values indicate reduced mobility; positive values indicate above-baseline movement. Use the state filter and time slider to isolate specific periods and regions.

title: Mobility analysis — Mexico (COVID-19 period)
text:

Aggregated mobility data from publicly available sources (2020). State-level analysis, Mexico.

title: Land use and vegetation change — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Mexico’s National Institute of Statistics and Geography (INEGI) has mapped national land use and vegetation cover six times since the 1970s — each survey classified at a consistent scale, making longitudinal comparison possible. This dashboard works through all six series for the Yucatán Peninsula, showing where forest cover has been lost, where agricultural land has expanded, and where urban and tourism development has converted coastal and inland ecosystems.
The peninsula sits atop a karst aquifer with no surface rivers. What happens on the land surface — what is grown, what is cleared, what is sealed with concrete — directly affects how much rainwater infiltrates to recharge the groundwater. That relationship is what makes land use change relevant to the water metabolism modelling in the Yucatán water project.

title: Land use and vegetation change — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
How to read it
Each survey period covers a different reference year. Use the filter controls to compare individual series or step through them in sequence. The colour scheme follows the INEGI classification: primary forest, secondary vegetation, agricultural land, urban, and other.

title: Land use and vegetation change — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
Data
INEGI, Land Use and Vegetation Series I through VI. Published by Mexico’s National Institute of Statistics and Geography.

title: COVID-19 mortality effect — Mexico
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Official COVID-19 death counts depend on testing capacity, reporting systems, and the criteria used to classify causes of death. In Mexico in 2020, questions arose early about whether deaths were being attributed to other causes — particularly viral pneumonia — in ways that understated the true COVID burden. This dashboard examines that question using the SEED (Sistema Epidemiológico y Estadístico de las Defunciones), the national death registry, which covers all recorded deaths going back to 1998.
By comparing 2020 mortality rates against the historical average for the same period, the excess mortality picture becomes visible regardless of how individual deaths were classified. The state-level breakdown reveals the uneven geography of the pandemic’s first wave: Mexico City was hit first and hardest, but several southern and central states with reporting delays showed elevated mortality months later.
The analysis connects to a longer write-up on the data.

title: COVID-19 mortality effect — Mexico
text:
How to read it
The main view shows mortality rates per 100,000 inhabitants by state and month. Use the historical comparison panel to see how 2020 diverged from the 1998–2019 average. The cause-of-death breakdown shows the proportion attributed to COVID-19 versus pneumonia and other respiratory causes.

title: COVID-19 mortality effect — Mexico
text:
Data
SEED (Sistema Epidemiológico y Estadístico de las Defunciones), SINAIS / DGIS, 1998–September 2020. Federal death registry, Mexico.

title: Climate-health hot spots in Brazilian agriculture
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Climate change affects agricultural workers first through their health. This dataset assembles two decades of occupational health surveillance records to make that connection visible at the municipality level across Brazil.
Two national systems contribute the data. SINAN (the notifiable diseases reporting system) provides incidence records from 2009 to 2024, covering work-related accidents, dengue cases, agrochemical poisoning, and violence among agricultural workers. SIHSUS (the national hospital admissions system) provides inpatient records from 2003 to 2024, filtered to approximately 900 ICD-10 diagnoses linked to six climate-driven occupational health pathways: excessive heat, air pollution, UV radiation, vector-borne diseases, agrochemical exposure, and waterborne diseases.
The dataset is the empirical foundation for the paper “Mapping Climate-Health Hot Spots: Implications for Workers’ Health and Decent Work in Brazilian Agriculture.”

title: Cleaning ThinkHazard! data for Brazil
text:
← Back to Portfolio
The World Bank’s ThinkHazard! tool gives researchers a quick read on natural hazard risk — river floods, earthquakes, droughts, cyclones, coastal floods, tsunamis, volcanoes, landslides — for any country or sub-national region. The raw data comes as a multi-sheet Excel file with numeric hazard codes instead of plain names, one worksheet per hazard type. Before any analysis can happen, that needs cleaning.
This script does it in one run.

title: Cleaning ThinkHazard! data for Brazil
text:
What it does
The script downloads the full ThinkHazard! dataset from the World Bank’s public portal, reads every worksheet, translates numeric hazard codes into readable names, filters to Brazil records, merges the sheets into a single flat table, and exports a CSV. No API key required.
It was built for the CLIDEWO project, where hazard risk data was the starting layer before modelling how climate exposure intersects with labour conditions in Brazilian agriculture.

title: Cleaning ThinkHazard! data for Brazil
text:
Run it
# Dependencies
install.packages(c("readxl", "dplyr", "data.table"))

# Run
source("thinkhazard_cleaning.R")
# → outputs brazil_thinkhazard.csv in the working directory
The full script is on GitHub.

title: Clidewo — climate, work and health in Brazil
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Brazilian agriculture employs millions of workers who are, by any reasonable measure, on the front line of climate change. Heat stress, flooding, and drought are getting worse. At the same time, labour rights violations in the sector are documented and ongoing. The CLIDEWO project tried to look at both problems together rather than separately – asking how climate exposure and labour vulnerability interact, and what the data actually show.
The project brought together researchers from the University of Nottingham, the Federal Fluminense University (UFF), the Federal University of Mato Grosso (UFMT), and the Federal University of Maranhão (UFMA).

title: Clidewo — climate, work and health in Brazil
text:
What the site contains
The website is the public output of the project. It has the datasets used in the analysis, interactive visualisations (including the Constellation of Inspections), the R packages built during the project with documentation, a bilingual blog in English and Portuguese with project updates and plain-language summaries, and links to the papers and working documents.

title: Clidewo — climate, work and health in Brazil
text:
The method
The team built a multi-layer climate risk and vulnerability assessment (CRVA) from climate model outputs, socio-economic data, and qualitative evidence from workers. The goal was a risk picture that is worker-informed, not just satellite-derived.

title: Workers’ Health under Climate Change: Agrochemicals
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Agricultural workers are regularly exposed to agrochemicals — pesticides, herbicides, fertilisers — through skin contact, inhalation, and contaminated food and water. Short-term effects include skin irritation, eye problems, headaches, and respiratory difficulty. Long-term exposure is linked to chronic respiratory disease, hormonal disruption, reproductive problems, nerve damage, and elevated cancer risk. Limited access to protective equipment and training amplifies these risks.

title: Workers’ Health under Climate Change: Agrochemicals
text:
Agrochemicals score
The agrochemicals score integrates two indicators:

Application of pesticides — sourced from Ormaza-Zulueta & Mehrabi (2025), Environmental Research Communications1
Pesticides risk score — sourced from Tang et al. (2021), Nature Geoscience2

The visualisation summarises agrochemical exposure risk in Brazil, disaggregated by municipality, population, and crop type, to identify spatial differences and exposure hotspots. By translating complex climate and socio-environmental data into accessible visual formats, it supports targeted interventions and risk-informed policy.

title: Climate vulnerability as a driver of forced-labour risk
text:
Abstract
I presented evidence from a cross-sectional analysis of 60 producing economies showing that climate-vulnerability indices are strongly correlated with downstream forced-labour risk in food-system supply chains, even after controlling for governance quality and HDI. The talk argued for treating climate adaptation and labour-rights protection as a single design problem.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
text:
← Back to Blog
I needed a research portfolio updated. That’s not a inspiring opening line, but it’s the honest reason I started this. I had papers to show, projects to link, and a CV that hasn’t been updated in months. I’d tried GitHub Pages + Quarto in the past, and it’s great!,you create a couple of templates and pages, and the structure is ready to be populated—it’s pretty simple. But it only gets the job done halfway; customizing it to my liking can take a long time, and aesthetic details like transitions or animations simply aren’t straightforward to implement. This is where Claude (Claudio para los amigos) intervienes.
On the one hand, it designs an aesthetically appealing, generic model for you.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
text:
Designing in Claude
I didn’t start by writing HTML. I described what I wanted in a Claude conversation — editorial typography, monochrome-to-color hover effects on data visualisations, the kind of layout you’d expect from a journal’s digital presence rather than a startup’s landing page.
Claude generated a React prototype as a single HTML file. I opened it in a browser immediately. The code itself was unusable in Quarto (React doesn’t fit a static publishing system), but that wasn’t the point. What I had was a visual reference with actual hex values, font size decisions, and a working grid structure. When I later asked Claude Code to style the Quarto output, I could point at a file rather than describe from memory.
That prototype file is still sitting in the repo. I open it alongside the live preview whenever I need to check whether something matches the intended design.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
text:
Translating to Quarto
Quarto turns markdown files into static HTML. The research-workflow appeal is obvious: write content, run quarto render, site rebuilds. No database. No CMS. Adding a new publication means creating a .qmd file. Adding a new conference talk means the same.
The structural translation from React prototype to Quarto took a few days. Design tokens became CSS custom properties. React components became Quarto’s fenced-div syntax (::: {.classname}). Navigation, footer, and layouts live in _quarto.yml.
The friction: Quarto has strong opinions about HTML output. It wraps content in <p> tags in unexpected places. It sits on top of Bootstrap, which has its own cascade. CSS selectors that matched perfectly in the prototype broke because Quarto’s listing system generates different class names than you’d assume.
I spent a lot of time reading the actual rendered HTML rather than trusting that the selector I wrote would work.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
text:
Content as files
Every publication, project, conference talk, and portfolio item is a .qmd file with YAML frontmatter:
---
title: "Quantifying modern slavery risk in photovoltaic supply chains"
date: 2024-06-15
categories: [Supply Chains, SoLCA, Networks]
image: ../assets/images/img-network.svg
---
Quarto’s listing system reads these files and generates index pages automatically. When a paper gets accepted, I create a file. I don’t touch any HTML. I don’t touch any CSS. The listing page updates on the next render.
This is the part that makes the site worth maintaining long-term. The design work is done once. The content side is just files.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
section: Claude Code for the complex parts
text:
Claude Code for the complex parts
Smooth page-exit animations, JavaScript that reads the current URL and decides which page comes next, CSS grid selectors that survive Quarto’s Bootstrap layer — I could have figured all of this out eventually. Claude Code did it faster.
The workflow for each of these: describe the specific behavior I wanted, get an implementation, render the site, check it in a browser, describe what was still wrong. The quality of the result tracked closely with how specific I was about the problem. “The transition looks fast” produced a longer duration. “The vertical movement feels mechanical and the easing cuts off abruptly at the end” produced a different timing curve and a changed easing function.
What Claude Code can’t do: tell you whether the output looks right. That part is still yours.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
text:
Browser DevTools for the details
quarto render, then open _site/index.html in a browser. Right-click anything that looks wrong. Inspect.
The computed styles panel shows exactly what CSS is applied and where it comes from. When a font rendered at the wrong size, I traced it through the cascade to find which rule was winning. When a grid card had unexpected padding, I found which Quarto class added it. When the hover animation wasn’t firing, I found that the selector I’d written matched zero elements.
That last one happened more than once. The Quarto listing system generates .quarto-listing-container-grid, .g-col-1, and .quarto-grid-item — not .quarto-listing-grid .listing-item, which is what I’d written by reading the documentation rather than the output. Twenty minutes with DevTools cleared up an hour of confusion.
The general principle: quarto render shows you what the code produces. DevTools shows you what the browser sees. The gap between those two is where most bugs live.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
section: What I’d tell myself at the start
text:
What I’d tell myself at the start
Get the prototype first. Trying to design directly in Quarto CSS is slow because the feedback loop is long and the rendered output is harder to reason about than a browser-native HTML file. Having a static reference — even a rough one — makes every subsequent conversation with Claude Code faster.
Read the rendered HTML. Not the .qmd source, not the styles.css file. The actual _site/ output. Quarto transforms things. Bootstrap overrides things. The only way to know what’s actually in the page is to look.
Describe behavior, not implementation. “Fade the page content before navigating” is more useful than “use GSAP with opacity 0 and y -12 over 0.24 seconds.” The implementation is Claude Code’s job. The behavior is yours to specify.

title: Designed in Claude, rebuilt it in Quarto and polishing using css
text:
The result
The site is easy to update and genuinely adaptable. All color tokens are CSS custom properties — switching the entire palette is a handful of hex values at the top of one file. Grid columns, type scale, spacing, and animation timing are all adjustable without touching content files.
For research, the maintenance cost is low. New paper, new file. New talk, new file. The design stays stable while the content grows.
The AI involvement didn’t remove the need to understand what was happening. I still needed to read generated HTML, trace selectors through DevTools, understand why a CSS rule wasn’t matching. What I didn’t have to do was start from zero on each problem. Claude handled the implementation. I handled the verification. That division turned out to be a reasonable one.
— Edgar

title: Data challenges in measuring hidden labour abuses in global supply chains
text:
Modern slavery is, by definition, hidden. The people perpetrating it have every incentive to keep it invisible. The people experiencing it are often unable to speak freely. And the data infrastructure we rely on — surveys, administrative records, trade statistics — was built to measure things that are meant to be counted.
This creates a fundamental methodological problem: how do you measure something that actively evades measurement?

title: Data challenges in measuring hidden labour abuses in global supply chains
text:
What data exists
There are three broad categories of data on forced labour in supply chains.
Prevalence estimates from organisations like Walk Free Foundation give national-level counts of people in modern slavery. These are constructed from household surveys, expert elicitation, and country-level risk modelling. They’re the best we have, but they carry enormous uncertainty — confidence intervals often span an order of magnitude.
Worker surveys — like those conducted by the ILO’s BEEP programme or sector-specific audits — give richer data on specific worksites or commodity chains. But they suffer from selection effects: audits happen at sites that consent to audits, which are systematically different from the sites most likely to have problems.
Indirect indicators — wage gaps, working hour violations, debt bondage markers — can be triangulated across multiple data sources. This is the approach my own work takes: building a composite signal from imperfect indicators rather than relying on any single measure.

title: Data challenges in measuring hidden labour abuses in global supply chains
text:
The structural problem
The deeper issue is that data gaps are not random. They cluster in exactly the places where exploitation is worst: informal economies, fragmented supply chains, countries with weak statistical capacity, sectors with high migrant labour concentration.
This means that our estimates of forced labour are systematically biased downward in the places that matter most. The absence of data is itself a signal.

title: Data challenges in measuring hidden labour abuses in global supply chains
text:
What this means for research
Any quantitative study of forced labour in supply chains should be explicit about what it cannot see, not just what it can. The error bars matter as much as the point estimates.
The methodological frontier is not better data collection (though that matters) — it is better models for reasoning under measurement-induced uncertainty.

title: Trade-offs of the Sustainable Development Goals
text:
The 17 Sustainable Development Goals were designed to be pursued together. The ambition is right. The challenge is that pursuing one goal often creates pressure on another, and the framework does not always make those trade-offs visible.
A simple example: SDG 8 (decent work and economic growth) depends on expanding economic activity in many low-income contexts. More economic activity means more resource consumption. More resource consumption tends to conflict with SDG 12 (responsible production and consumption) and SDG 13 (climate action). The goals are not wrong, but treating them as isolated targets means missing the relationships between them.
Getting past that requires a systemic approach. Several analytical methods have been developed for studying SDG interactions directly: system dynamics and causal diagrams, nexus analyses linking water, energy, food, and land use, and approaches from complexity science and societal metabolism. Each has advantages and limitations. None of them is simple to apply across the 169 targets, four geographic scales, and the different cultural and institutional contexts that matter for implementation.
The complexity is real and it is not an argument for doing less. It is an argument for being precise about what is being proposed and what it is likely to affect. A policy that advances SDG 6 (clean water) while degrading SDG 2 (food security) is not automatically net positive. Understanding the direction and magnitude of those spillovers is what allows for better design.
The methods for doing this analysis are there. The harder part is building research and policy communities that are willing to use them before they announce the targets.

title: Rodríguez-Huerta, Edgar
text:
02 — About

Bridging disciplines, seeing the system




I’m a researcher at the University of Nottingham’s Rights Lab, working on how climate change, supply chains, and modern slavery connect. My day-to-day involves a mix of quantitative methods: climate modelling, network analysis, supply-chain mapping, and life cycle assessment. I’ve applied these across quite different industries, from food systems and solar panels to brick kilns.
I got here by a roundabout path. I trained as an Industrial Engineer and spent my early career as a business consultant, mostly building dashboards and performance metrics. Somewhere along the way I started asking what we were actually measuring, and whether profit was the right scorecard. That question pulled me toward sustainability research, and I’ve stayed there.
The part I enjoy most is making technical findings legible to people outside the field. A good chart or map can open up a conversation that a 40-page report won’t.

title: COVID-19 and mortality rates in Mexico: what the data shows
text:
During the early months of the pandemic in Mexico, there were questions about whether deaths were being reclassified under other causes to reduce the official COVID-19 count. The Epidemiological and Statistical System of Deaths (SEED) covers 1998 onwards and provides a basis for comparing 2020 mortality against historical trends. That comparison is what this analysis looked at.
A few things stood out. Several states showed reporting delays through mid-2020, particularly Oaxaca, Michoacán, Guerrero, Puebla, and Jalisco. By June 2020, the national mortality rate had reached roughly 70 deaths per 100,000 inhabitants — a 30% increase above the same period in previous years.
Mexico City showed the sharpest departure from historical norms. The rate nearly tripled to around 160 deaths per 100,000, and approximately one in four deaths in the city involved COVID-19. Nationally, COVID-19 accounted for about 11% of total deaths as of the end of June.
The reclassification question: approximately 2,300 deaths involving COVID-related causes were recorded under different diagnoses. The category “J129 — Viral Pneumonia, Unspecified” showed around 300 additional cases compared to the same period in previous years. That is not an enormous number in absolute terms, but it points to the kind of reclassification that can understate the burden in official counts.
The data does not answer whether this was intentional. Reporting systems under sudden pressure make errors that can look like suppression. What it does show is that excess mortality is a more reliable signal than confirmed case counts during a period when testing capacity is limited and reporting pipelines are strained.

title: The hidden cost of renewables: forced labour in Brazil’s ethanol supply chain
text:
Brazil’s sugarcane ethanol is often presented as one of the more successful examples of large-scale renewable fuel production. It has been running for decades, it has displaced a meaningful share of petrol consumption, and it has attracted genuine interest from countries looking for models of bioenergy transition. That is one version of the story.
The other version is documented in the “Enslaved in Ethanol” report by Repórter Brasil. Workers in the sugarcane sector face debt bondage created by employer-imposed deductions for housing, food, and transport. They work in conditions of extreme physical exhaustion — in some documented cases, sleeping around four hours a night. Pesticide exposure happens with inadequate protection, including during aerial spraying. Many workers lack formal contracts, which means no access to social security and no recourse when something goes wrong.
This matters for how we talk about the energy transition. A fuel’s carbon intensity does not capture what it costs the people who produce it. If we decarbonise the energy sector while pushing the human cost of that decarbonisation onto workers who are already among the most vulnerable — informal, rural, migrant — then we have not actually solved anything. We have shifted the problem.
The arguments for stronger accountability are not complicated. Labour law enforcement needs to reach agricultural workers, not just workers in formal industrial settings. Companies that buy ethanol need to know where it comes from and what conditions were involved in producing it. That requires transparency, which requires supply chain mapping, which is part of what projects like CLIDEWO are trying to build.
Environmental progress and labour rights are not separate problems with separate solution sets. They are the same problem, and the response has to treat them that way.
CLIDEWO Project, University of Nottingham Rights Lab.

title: LEGO-tellying: research communication through creative storytelling
text:
As part of the Research Communication program, Mastering the Art of Research Communication: Develop your Skills! by the University of Nottingham’s Research Academy, we had the opportunity to disseminate our projects using non-conventional resources to change mindsets and encourage creativity. It was enjoyable to revisit LEGO — now to continue exploring new ways to share research. We are working to produce a worker-informed, data-driven climate risk and vulnerability assessment (CRVA) for agricultural workers in Brazil. Each coloured LEGO layer represents a vital piece of the puzzle: climate data, socioeconomic conditions, and workers’ voices. By applying a multi-layer risk assessment, the project will provide insights into the relationship between climate change, workers’ health, and decent work. A big thank you to Andrew Rowe for introducing such a great initiative.  Sorry for the terrible map, but I ran out of time and green blocks 😅

title: Constellation of Inspections
text:
← Back to Portfolio
The DataViz focuses on results of labour inspections across Brazil, where the relevance behind each fiscalização becomes visible at a glance. Whether one rescue or 1,064, each star symbolises justice for workers — and a reminder of the immense task ahead, the territory still to be covered, and the need to strengthen inspection capacity.
More inspectors mean a greater capacity to identify spots of vulnerability and eradicate degrading working conditions.

title: Constellation of Inspections
text:
How to read it
Each point in the constellation represents one labour inspection (fiscalização) conducted by Brazil’s Ministry of Labour and Employment. Point size is proportional to the number of workers rescued from forced labour or degrading conditions during that inspection.
Voids in the constellation are not empty space — they are uncharted territory, regions where inspections have not yet reached. The density of points reflects inspection capacity as much as it reflects the actual distribution of labour violations.

title: Constellation of Inspections
text:
Data
Labour inspection records — Ministry of Labour and Employment, Brazil (MTPE / SIT). Part of the CLIDEWO project on climate change, decent work, and workers’ health in Brazilian agriculture.

title: Climate–Decent Work Risk Assessment
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Which regions experience the highest extreme heat exposure combined with excessive working hours? Which population groups face the most severe decent-work deficits? Which crops carry the highest agrochemical risk?
The dashboard lets you combine climate hazards and decent work indicators to answer your own questions.
Our assessment adapts the IPCC climate risk framework — hazards × exposure × vulnerability — to examine climate risks through a decent work lens. Decent work is treated as a multi-dimensional concept comprising ten core elements linked to the five strategic pillars of the ILO’s decent work agenda.

title: Climate–Decent Work Risk Assessment
text:
Climate hazards covered
The assessment covers five climate hazards with documented links to occupational health and decent work outcomes: extreme heat, flooding, drought, wildfires, and agrochemical exposure. Each hazard is scored at the municipality level for Brazil, then crossed with worker exposure and sectoral vulnerability data.

title: Climate–Decent Work Risk Assessment
text:
Decent work dimensions
Ten decent work elements are included, organised around the ILO’s five strategic pillars: employment opportunities, rights at work, social protection, social dialogue, and conditions of work. Measurable indicators include wages, job security, working hours, occupational safety, and access to formal employment contracts.

title: Climate–Decent Work Risk Assessment
text:
About CLIDEWO
This dashboard is a core output of the CLIDEWO project — Climate Change, Decent Work and Workers’ Health: Vulnerabilities and Response in Brazilian Agriculture — funded by the British Academy’s ODA Challenge-Oriented Research Grants programme.

title: Worker risk training module — India
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Research on forced labour in Indian brick kilns and garment factories is not much use if it never reaches the workers it describes. This module was built to close some of that gap. It lays out the environmental and labour risks workers in those industries face – heat, chemical exposure, dust, flooding, rights violations – in plain language and in the languages workers actually speak.
Four versions exist: English, Hindi, Punjabi, and Bengali. The geographic focus covers the brick kiln belt running through Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, and West Bengal, and garment manufacturing hubs in the same region.

title: Worker risk training module — India
text:
What it covers
The module explains what the research found about hazard exposure and forced labour risk in these sectors, what constitutes a rights violation under Indian labour law, and what routes exist for workers and community organisations to raise concerns. It draws on the University of Nottingham’s Slavery from Space project and the broader India brick kilns research programme.

title: Climate and decent work risk — Brazilian municipalities
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Most climate risk datasets stop at the environment. Most labour datasets stop at the workplace. This one tries to hold both at the same time, covering all 5,570 Brazilian municipalities at 0.05-degree spatial resolution.
The structure follows the IPCC risk framework: Risk = Hazard × Exposure × Vulnerability. The climate side draws on 23 indicators across nine hazard categories. The decent work side draws on 35 indicators covering ten ILO decent work elements. Both sides are normalized to a 0–100 scale using expert-weighted priors adjusted by Data Quality Assessment scores, so they can be compared and combined.

title: Climate and decent work risk — Brazilian municipalities
text:
Climate hazard indicators
Nine categories: heat stress, UV radiation, drought, river and coastal flooding, wildfires, air pollution, vector-borne diseases (dengue, malaria, leishmaniasis), agrochemical exposure, and WASH (water, sanitation, and hygiene) access. Primary sources include CCVI v2.0, TEMIS UV, World Bank ThinkHazard!, and AdaptaBrasil.

title: Climate and decent work risk — Brazilian municipalities
text:
Decent work indicators
Ten ILO decent work elements: employment opportunities, earnings adequacy, working conditions, worker rights, social protection, social dialogue, labour inspections, gender equality, informality, and child/forced labour. Drawn from PNADC (2021–2024) and related national surveys.

title: Climate and decent work risk — Brazilian municipalities
text:
Coverage and linkage
The dataset covers Brazil’s 5,570 municipalities. Records include 7-digit IBGE codes so they can be linked to any other municipal-level dataset.
Agricultural exposure is built from approximately 113 crop types (harvested area data) cross-referenced with worker demographics from the agricultural census.

title: Forced labour risk in the US food supply — replication data
text:
← Back to Portfolio
This is the replication dataset for Blackstone et al. (2023), “Forced labor risk is pervasive in the US land-based food supply,” published in Nature Food. It contains the supply chain shares, risk scores, and data quality assessments used to produce the paper’s estimates.
The core question the paper addresses is straightforward: if you trace every ingredient in a US food item back to where it was grown or raised, how much forced labour risk accumulates along the way? The data here supports that calculation for a wide range of land-based food commodities.

title: Forced labour risk in the US food supply — replication data
text:
What the dataset contains
Supply data comes from the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), adjusted to represent each supplier country’s share at upstream levels of the supply chain. Risk scores are derived from multiple qualitative-to-quantitative conversion methods documented in the paper’s methods section. Each record also carries a data quality assessment score so users can see how much confidence the estimates warrant.

title: Actual evapotranspiration — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Evapotranspiration is the combined loss of water from the land surface through direct evaporation from soil and water bodies and transpiration from vegetation. In a water balance, it competes directly with infiltration — water that evapotranspires cannot recharge the aquifer. The Yucatán Peninsula’s tropical climate means evapotranspiration losses are large and strongly seasonal: the dry season (November–April) concentrates nearly all the ET demand into a period when rainfall is sparse.
This dashboard shows actual evapotranspiration (ETa) rather than potential ET (ETp). Actual ET is limited by available water — in the dry season, soil moisture runs out before the atmosphere’s demand does, so ETa falls below ETp. The distinction matters for the water balance: a model that uses ETp in the dry season will underestimate recharge by assuming more water is consumed than actually is.
Monthly ETa values were estimated from the water balance model using FAO-56 Penman–Monteith reference ET and crop coefficient adjustments for the peninsula’s major land use types.

title: Actual evapotranspiration — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
How to read it
The map shows average annual ETa in mm·year⁻¹. Use the month selector to step through the seasonal cycle. The time series panel shows how ETa tracked alongside rainfall in the historical record.

title: Actual evapotranspiration — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
Methods
Part of the doctoral water balance modelling described in the Yucatán water project. ETa estimated from a calibrated monthly water balance model (1961–2000 historical period, four GCM projections for 2015–2039).

title: Forced labour risk in the US food supply
text:
← Back to Portfolio
In 2023, Blackstone et al. published a paper in Nature Food showing that forced labour risk is present across a wide range of commodities in the US land-based food supply – not just in the sectors most people would guess. These visualisations were built to let readers dig into those findings by commodity, sourcing country, and supply chain stage, rather than stopping at the summary numbers in the paper.
The work is part of the LASTING project at Tufts University on sustainability transitions in global food systems.

title: Forced labour risk in the US food supply
text:
What you can explore
The interactive dashboards let you filter and compare forced labour risk scores across food categories (grains, fruit, vegetables, meat, dairy), break them down by the countries where ingredients are sourced, and see where in the supply chain – farm, processing, wholesale – risk is concentrated.
Risk scores reflect estimated prevalence of forced labour conditions in the supply chains behind US food consumption. A higher score means the data points to greater probability of forced labour somewhere upstream, not that violations are confirmed at a specific site.

text:

title: Social-ecological metabolism and just transition — country profiles
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Solar energy is expanding rapidly, and the raw materials and manufacturing capacity that make it possible are concentrated in a small number of countries — many with documented labour rights risks. The just transition question is whether the shift to renewables can happen without embedding new forms of exploitation in the supply chains that make it possible.
This dashboard profiles key producer countries across a set of social-ecological sustainability indicators: labour rights, governance quality, environmental regulation, energy mix, and selected social sustainability metrics. The aim is to give researchers and policymakers a quick comparative view of where countries sit on these dimensions simultaneously, rather than looking at environmental performance and social performance in isolation.
The work supported the “Solar Energy, Modern Slavery, and the Just Transition” report funded by the British Academy.

title: Social-ecological metabolism and just transition — country profiles
text:
How to read it
Select a country to see its full profile. The radar view shows relative performance across all indicator dimensions. The comparison mode lets you place two countries side by side.

title: Social-ecological metabolism and just transition — country profiles
text:
Data
International Labour Organisation, World Bank governance indicators, national energy statistics, Walk Free Foundation Global Slavery Index, and supplementary sources. Data primarily 2019–2021.

title: Social-ecological metabolism and just transition — country profiles
text:
Related
British Academy report: The Energy of Freedom? Solar Energy, Modern Slavery and the Just Transition (2022).

title: Water use vs groundwater recharge — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
← Back to Portfolio
This is the core output visualisation from the doctoral research on water societal metabolism in the Yucatán Peninsula. It puts supply and demand on the same axis: how much water naturally recharges the karst aquifer through rainfall infiltration each year, and how much the economy — agriculture, tourism, industry, households — extracts from it.
The two lines can be read like a scissors: recharge is falling under most climate projections (around 23% less by 2030 under central estimates), while demand is growing as the economy shifts toward water-intensive activities. The point where demand approaches or exceeds recharge is not a theoretical endpoint but a near-term planning constraint in some sub-regions.

title: Water use vs groundwater recharge — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
How to read it
The balance view shows the gap between supply and demand by sub-region and scenario. The scenario explorer lets you toggle between different combinations of economic development pathway and climate trajectory. Negative balances — demand exceeding recharge — are shown in red.

title: Water use vs groundwater recharge — Yucatán Peninsula
text:
Methods
Recharge estimated from a monthly water balance model calibrated against 40 years of historical data, then projected using four downscaled General Circulation Models. Demand estimated via MuSIASEM (Multi-Scale Integrated Analysis of Societal and Ecosystem Metabolism) using employment structure, water intensity coefficients, and demographic projections.
Full methodology in: Rodríguez-Huerta, E., Rosas-Casals, M. & Hernández-Terrones, L.M. (2019). Journal of Cleaner Production, 235, 272–287. doi:10.1016/j.jclepro.2019.06.310

title: What-a-Crop — edible crop explorer
text:
← Back to Portfolio
Food system analysis often treats “crops” as a homogeneous category, or focuses narrowly on the handful of staples (wheat, rice, maize, soy) that dominate global trade. The EDIBLE project takes a different starting point: the full diversity of what is actually grown and eaten, from globally traded commodities to regional staples that barely appear in FAO statistics.
This dashboard was built to make that diversity legible. It lets you filter and compare edible crops by production region, nutritional profile, production volume, and category (cereals, pulses, fruits, vegetables, oilseeds, etc.). The aim is to support research and policy conversations that go beyond the headline commodities — particularly in contexts where biodiversity in food production matters for resilience and for understanding local food system sustainability.

title: What-a-Crop — edible crop explorer
text:
How to read it
Use the category and region filters to narrow the crop set. The comparison view puts selected crops side by side on nutritional and production indicators. The map view shows which crops are produced where, at what scale.

title: What-a-Crop — edible crop explorer
text:
Data
FAO crop production statistics and nutritional composition data. Built for the EDIBLE project (May 2019 version).

title: Brick Kilns - Agriculture connecttion
text: ← Back to Projects

title: Brick Kilns - Agriculture connecttion
text:
Overview
Brick kilns across South Asia employ an estimated 10–15 million workers, the majority under conditions that meet international definitions of bonded or forced labour. Seasonal production cycles, advance debt payments to migrant workers, and isolated rural worksites create structural conditions for labour exploitation that persist across India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Pakistan despite extensive legal prohibitions.
This project applies a societal metabolism approach alongside social sustainability frameworks to analyse how bonded labour conditions are embedded in and reproduced by the material and economic flows of brick production. By quantifying labour inputs alongside material and energy flows, we examine how production scale, seasonal demand, and supply-chain structure interact with worker vulnerability.
The project also produced an open educational resource, developed with the University of Nottingham, examining environmental and social impacts in the brick kiln sector.
“Follow the labour”

title: The Energy of Freedom? Solar Energy, Modern Slavery and the Just Transition
text: ← Back to Projects

title: The Energy of Freedom? Solar Energy, Modern Slavery and the Just Transition
text:
Overview
Is solar energy “the energy of freedom” — or does it, in fact, put that freedom at risk for the workers who produce it? Around 40–45 % of global polysilicon is manufactured in China’s Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region under what multiple governments and independent tribunals have characterised as state-sponsored forced labour. A further 15–30 % of the cobalt used in lithium-ion battery storage originates from artisanal mines in the Democratic Republic of Congo where forced and child labour are prevalent.
This project — conducted for the British Academy’s Just Transitions within Sectors and Industries Globally programme — develops a new, scalable method for estimating forced labour risk across the photovoltaic (PV) supply chain, from raw silica extraction to consumer markets. We demonstrate how modern slavery risks can be quantified at the country-level production system, identifying where in the value chain risk accumulates and how sensitive the system is to changes in upstream conditions.


Project lead James Cockayne


Co-investigators Edgar Rodríguez-Huerta · Oana Burcu


Funder: British Academy — Just Transitions within Sectors and Industries Globally Period: November 2021 – March 2022 Host institution: Rights Lab, University of Nottingham

title: The Energy of Freedom? Solar Energy, Modern Slavery and the Just Transition
section: Method: Social Life Cycle Assessment of PV supply chains
text:
Method: Social Life Cycle Assessment of PV supply chains
This project applies a Social Life Cycle Assessment (S-LCA) framework to the global solar energy value chain — to our knowledge the first multi-country S-LCA in the renewable energy sector. Rather than measuring environmental impact per unit of product (as in conventional LCA), we measure forced labour risk as it cascades through the economic relationships that produce one kilowatt-hour of on-grid PV electricity.

Data integration
The method combines four independent datasets:

World Bank / IRENA — levelised cost of electricity (LCOE) by country and by supply-chain breakdown (PV module and inverter, balance-of-system hardware, installation, soft costs, O&M). LCOE provides the functional unit for disaggregating cost — and therefore risk — across the life cycle.
UN Comtrade — bilateral trade flows used to trace where each input into a country’s PV production system originates. This is the economic input–output component (EIO-LCA): large trade flows carry proportionally larger shares of the risk embedded in each source country.
PSILCA database — the Product Social Impact Life Cycle Assessment database, version 3, which provides 88 social impact indicators for every country-specific sector (CSS) combination available in the EORA Multi-Regional Input–Output (MRIO) database (187 countries; 15,909 sectors). Risk scores reflect 2018 data, updated for key CSS components using 2022 US Department of Labor data.
Walk Free Foundation Global Slavery Index — underpins the frequency-of-forced-labour indicator.



Forced labour indicators
Three indicators score each CSS component in the value chain:








Indicator
Source
What it captures




FFL — Frequency of Forced Labour
Walk Free Foundation Global Slavery Index
Estimated proportion of a country’s population in modern slavery


GFL — Goods produced by Forced Labour
US Dept of Labor (ILAB) list
Number of commodity classes in a sector produced in whole or in part by forced labour


TP — Trafficking in Persons
US State Dept annual TIP Report tier rankings
Country-level anti-trafficking enforcement



These combine into a composite Forced Labour Index (FLI):
\[\text{FLI}_i = 0.30 \times \text{FFL}_{\text{scaled},i} + 0.60 \times \text{GFL}_{\text{scaled},i} + 0.10 \times \text{TP}_{\text{scaled},i}\]
GFL receives the highest weight because it is most directly linked to specific goods in the supply chain; FFL captures broader population-level risk; TP proxies institutional enforcement capacity.


Output metrics
The FLI is translated into two final measures, applicable to any energy source:

FLR/kWh — forced labour risk in medium risk hour equivalents (mrh-eq) embodied in one kilowatt-hour of on-grid PV electricity, from cradle to gate.
FLR/USD LCOE — the same risk expressed per dollar of levelised electricity cost, useful for buyer and investor comparison.

Both metrics can be applied to other energy sources, opening a path towards forced labour risk integration into ESG benchmarking and energy market comparisons.

title: The Energy of Freedom? Solar Energy, Modern Slavery and the Just Transition
text:
Key findings
Country-level results (top 30 producers, 96 % of global output)

China and India show the highest FLR/kWh scores on the GFL indicator, driven by the high volume of goods produced with forced labour in their PV hardware supply chains.
India and Ukraine show elevated FFL scores, reflecting broader national prevalence of forced labour across their economic sectors.
When risk is expressed as FLR/USD LCOE — the metric most relevant to buyers — India slightly exceeds China due to its higher solar LCOE.

Value-chain structure of risk

For GFL (goods-based risk), the primary driver is PV module and inverter hardware — particularly Chinese modules, which are both high-GFL and a large share of global LCOE.
For FFL (population-level risk), the highest mean arises in “Other soft costs” and O&M — sectors with large labour inputs across many countries.
Upstream components account for 55 % of FFL risk, 30 % of GFL risk, and 69 % of TP risk across the 30 countries studied.

Sensitivity analysis — system-level significance
A single CSS component — CN-Other electric machinery and equipment (the polysilicon production sector in China) — intervenes in 95 % of national PV value chains studied. Shifting its FFL risk score from “very low” (reflecting 2018 data, before Xinjiang allegations emerged) to “very high” produces a 15,385 % increase in mean FFL risk across all 30 countries (from 0.00026 to 0.04 mrh-eq/kWh). A change in one upstream node reverberates through nearly every solar energy system on the planet.

title: The Energy of Freedom? Solar Energy, Modern Slavery and the Just Transition
text:
Policy implications
The study argues that a scalable S-LCA method could:

Allow firms and investors to identify higher-risk CSS components for targeted due diligence, rather than resource-intensive audits across the entire supply chain.
Enable integration of forced labour risk into ESG benchmarks, sustainability-linked finance, and energy procurement standards.
Support policy actors in setting risk thresholds or milestones tied to forced labour metrics rather than arbitrary cut-off dates.
Be replicated for battery supply chains (cobalt) and extended to other product systems beyond energy.

Interactive country risk profiles for all 30 countries are available via Tableau Public.

title: Forced Labour Risk in the US Food Supply
text: ← Back to Projects

title: Forced Labour Risk in the US Food Supply
text:
Overview
Forced labour risk in global food systems is routinely framed as a problem of distant supply chains — exports from low-income countries reaching wealthy consumers. This programme of research challenges that assumption. Across two linked studies published in Nature Food, we show that the majority of forced labour risk embedded in the US food supply originates domestically, and that the foods most frequently recommended as healthy or sustainable carry their own, underappreciated labour rights burden.
The work develops and applies a Social Life Cycle Assessment (S-LCA) framework capable of resolving forced labour risk at the commodity, supply-chain stage, and dietary-pattern level — producing the first comprehensive forced labour risk profile of the US land-based food system.

title: Forced Labour Risk in the US Food Supply
section: Study 1: Forced labour risk is pervasive in the US land-based food supply
text:
Study 1: Forced labour risk is pervasive in the US land-based food supply
Blackstone, Rodríguez-Huerta, Battaglia et al. — Nature Food, 2023

What we did
We assessed forced labour risk across the entire US land-based food supply, covering 1,312 product–country combinations at the commodity stage and 2,661 activity–country combinations across processing. Risk was scored using the medium risk hours equivalent (mrh-eq) metric — a standard unit from Social LCA that expresses the volume of labour worked under forced conditions, weighted by risk severity.
Data integration drew on:

US Department of Labor ILAB List of Goods Produced by Child or Forced Labour
FAO trade and food supply accounts
Working hours data from the Social Hotspots Database
Hand-harvest risk assessments and over 700 investigative journalism articles on specific commodities and supply chains
NGO and government enforcement reports

Risk scores were assigned hierarchically: commodity-specific evidence where available (18 % of combinations), sector-specific data (49 %), and country-level background risk (33 %).


Key findings

62 % of total forced labour risk in the US food supply originates from domestic production or processing — not imports.
Animal-based proteins account for 28 % of total risk; discretionary foods (cocoa, coffee, refined sugar) for 23 %; processed fruits and vegetables for 18 %.
Geographically: USA (62 %), China (13 %, primarily apple juice concentrate), Mexico (8 %, fresh produce).
Agriculture carries 85 % of the embedded risk; processing the remaining 15 %.
The highest-risk individual commodities are cocoa powder and cake, shelled cashews, refined sugar, boneless beef, and apple juice concentrate.

The finding that domestic production dominates US forced labour risk contradicts the prevailing policy assumption that supply-chain due diligence need only focus on imports from low-income countries.

title: Forced Labour Risk in the US Food Supply
section: Study 2: Current and recommended diets in the USA have embedded forced labour risk
text:
Study 2: Current and recommended diets in the USA have embedded forced labour risk
Rodríguez-Huerta, Bell, Blackstone et al. — Nature Food, 2025

What we did
Building on the 2023 supply-chain framework, we linked forced labour risk estimates to nationally representative dietary intake data from NHANES (2015–2018), covering more than 200 food commodities. We then compared risk across five dietary patterns:

Current average US diet
Three patterns from the 2020–2025 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (Healthy US-Style, Healthy Mediterranean-Style, Healthy Vegetarian)
EAT-Lancet Planetary Health Diet

Risk scores incorporated both agricultural production and animal feed supply chains for livestock products.


Key findings
Forced labour risk (mrh-eq per capita per day) across dietary patterns:



Dietary pattern
FLR (mrh-eq/capita/day)




EAT-Lancet Planetary Health
0.546 — lowest

The Mediterranean-Style pattern — widely promoted for health and environmental benefits — carries the highest forced labour risk of all patterns tested, driven by protein foods, dairy, and fruits (especially avocados and cashews).
Plant-forward patterns show lower risk, but no recommended dietary pattern is free of embedded forced labour.
Protein foods, dairy, and fruits are the primary risk drivers across all patterns.

The results demonstrate that dietary guidance developed for health and environmental goals does not automatically reduce labour rights burdens — and may increase them.

title: Forced Labour Risk in the US Food Supply
text:
Methods (shared framework)
Both studies use a consistent S-LCA methodology grounded in the mrh-eq metric:
\[\text{mrh-eq} = \sum_i (\text{Risk characterization factor}_i \times \text{Labour hours}_i)\]
where the risk characterization factor for each activity–country combination reflects the severity and prevalence of forced labour conditions, and labour hours are derived from working-time data scaled to commodity production volumes.
The framework allows attribution of embedded forced labour risk to specific foods, supply-chain stages, and source countries — enabling both system-wide analysis and targeted policy recommendations.

title: Forced Labour Risk in the US Food Supply
text:
Significance
This work establishes that:

Forced labour in food systems is not a distant problem for importing countries — it is structurally embedded in domestic agricultural and processing sectors.
Dietary recommendations optimised for health or environmental sustainability do not automatically minimise social harms.
S-LCA applied at scale can inform procurement policy, labelling initiatives, and corporate human rights due diligence in ways that commodity-by-commodity investigation cannot.
